title: The feira livre: a weekly neighborhood institution
bodyMd:
In almost every Brazilian city, a stretch of street closes to cars once or twice a week and fills with stalls selling produce, cheese, fish, flowers, and household goods — this is a **feira livre**. It isn't a special event; it's a fixture, tied to that specific street on that specific day, year-round.

Each neighborhood has its own fixed schedule — 'a feira da Rua X é toda terça e sábado' is the kind of thing locals just know, the way you'd know a store's opening hours. Ask a neighbor or doorman (porteiro) which day and street your local feira happens on; it's rarely posted anywhere official, it's just common knowledge passed around.

title: How haggling actually works at a feira
bodyMd:
Forget the image of aggressive market bargaining — haggling at a Brazilian feira is mild, friendly, and mostly about **buying more, not paying less for the same thing**. Asking 'faz um precinho?' (can you give me a little discount?) on a normal-sized purchase is fine, but the bigger lever is volume: buy three kilos instead of one, or a full box instead of a handful, and the price per kilo naturally drops.

A vendor who says no to a discount isn't being rude, and pushing hard after that is considered poor form. The whole exchange is meant to stay light and personable — a shared joke, not a standoff.

title: Becoming a freguês: the payoff of going back
bodyMd:
Brazilians tend to pick one or two feirantes per stall type — one for fruit, one for fish, one for cheese — and stick with them for years. Once you're a recognized **freguês/freguesa** (regular customer), you'll often get first pick of the best produce, a little extra thrown in for free ('vai de brinde'), or a better price without even asking.

This loyalty runs both ways: a good feirante remembers what you usually buy, sets aside something they think you'll like, and treats you a shade more informally than a first-time customer. It's built slowly, over weeks and months of showing up, not something you can shortcut on a single visit.

title: Cash norms: bring small bills, and don't count on cards everywhere
bodyMd:
While Pix has made huge inroads at feiras — most stalls now display a QR code — cash is still king, and having **small bills and coins (trocado)** matters more than at a supermarket. A feirante making change for a customer every few minutes can genuinely run short of small notes early in the morning.

Showing up with a large bill for a small purchase can put a vendor in a bind, especially at the start of the day. If you can, break large notes at a padaria or lanchonete before heading to the feira, and keep some coins on hand for small buys.

title: Timing: go early for freshness, go late for a deal
bodyMd:
A feira livre usually runs from early morning until early-to-mid afternoon, and the two ends of that window serve very different goals. **Early morning** (right when stalls open) is when the produce is at its freshest and the selection is widest — this is when locals do their real weekly shopping.

**Near closing time**, feirantes would rather sell out than pack unsold produce back into the truck, so prices on that day's remaining stock often drop — sometimes sharply on things like leafy greens and ripe fruit that won't keep until the next feira day. Neither time is 'wrong', but know which trade-off you're making: best quality and choice early, or the better bargain late.

title: The small talk that comes before the transaction
bodyMd:
At a feira, even a quick purchase usually opens with a greeting and often a question — 'o que tá fresco hoje?' or a comment on the weather — before getting to the price. Jumping straight to 'quanto custa' with no greeting can come across as brusque, even if the interaction itself is fast.

This small talk isn't wasted time; it's part of how a feirante sizes you up as a potential freguês and decides how much to volunteer — a tip on ripeness, a recommendation, an extra piece tossed in. A friendly 'bom dia' costs nothing and tends to be repaid.
